text: Not all cognitive training transfers equally. A critical distinction in neuroplasticity research is the difference between near transfer — improvement on tasks closely resembling the training task — and far transfer, improvement on unrelated abilities. Commercial brain-training programs typically show near transfer only, which is why someone who trains on a memory game gets better at that game but not at real-world attention. Meditation and focused-attention practices appear distinct in this regard: because they train the meta-cognitive act of noticing and redirecting attention itself, the skill generalizes across contexts. You are not learning a task; you are strengthening the mechanism by which any task is approached.
question: Why might meditation show broader transfer than commercial brain-training programs, according to the passage?

options.text: Meditation activates more brain regions simultaneously than memory games do
Commercial programs rely on near transfer, while meditation is scientifically validated
Meditation trains the meta-cognitive act of noticing and redirecting attention itself, not a specific task
Brain-training programs produce cortical thickening that interferes with general focus
